pipeline.name: huu Refactor Plan
pipeline.description: Characterization-test baseline → per-file smell catalog → top-5 ranking by smell-weight × churn → a static Mikado graph per target → Fowler-grounded recommendations. Report-only.

steps.name: 1. Capture baseline + scaffold .huu/audits/refactor.md
steps.prompt:
You are huu's refactoring-planner bootstrap agent. Goal: capture the current test baseline (characterization tests), scaffold `.huu/audits/refactor.md`, initialize `.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json`.

=== STEP 0 — REPORT-ONLY HARD RULE ===
You may NOT modify any file in the repo OTHER than the audit artifacts under `.huu/audits/` (and `.huu/audits/.tmp/` for working files) plus the single `.gitignore` adjustment described below. The deliverable is a PLAN, not a refactor. NEVER touch source code, NEVER touch package.json / requirements.txt / pyproject.toml / Cargo.toml / lockfiles. Create the output directory with `mkdir -p .huu/audits/.tmp/refactor` before writing.

=== PERSISTENCE CHECK ===
The deliverables under `.huu/audits/` must survive the stage merge, so they cannot be gitignored.
Run: `git check-ignore -q .huu/audits && echo IGNORED || echo OK`
If IGNORED: the committed `.gitignore` excludes `.huu/`. Apply the MINIMAL rewrite — replace the line `.huu/` (or `.huu`) with `.huu/*` and add `!.huu/audits/` on the next line. Git cannot re-include below an excluded DIRECTORY, but `.huu/*` only excludes the entries, so the negation works. This one-line edit is the ONLY permitted change outside `.huu/audits/` in this pipeline. If OK: leave `.gitignore` alone.

=== STEP 1 — Locate the test runner ===
Look for `huu-tests.md` first (created by the huu Test Suite pipeline). If absent, detect the runner heuristically:
- Node: `npm test`, `npm run test`, `yarn test`, `pnpm test`. Look at package.json scripts.
- Python: `pytest`, `python -m unittest discover`.
- Go: `go test ./...`.
- Rust: `cargo test`.
- Java: `mvn test` or `gradle test`.
- Ruby: `bundle exec rspec` / `bundle exec rake test`.

If no test runner can be identified: write `.huu/audits/refactor.md` with a critical-severity warning at the top: "No tests detected. Refactoring without a safety net is dangerous. Recommend running the huu Test Suite pipeline first." Skip to step 3 (still build the smell catalog, but the Mikado plan will note "blocked: no characterization tests").

=== STEP 2 — Capture the baseline ===
Run the test command and capture:
- Exit code.
- Number of tests passing / failing / skipped.
- Total runtime.

If tests pass: this is the characterization baseline. If tests already fail: STILL proceed — note in the FAQ that baseline was red, and any refactoring recommendation must be conditioned on "fix the failing tests first".

Why this gate exists (Feathers, "Working Effectively with Legacy Code"): characterization tests "document your system's actual behavior, not the behavior you wish it had" — once in production, the system IS its own specification, including bug-as-feature behavior users depend on. Refactoring without them means changes to behavior go undetected.

=== STEP 3 — Write .huu/audits/refactor.md scaffold ===
Path: `./.huu/audits/refactor.md`.

# .huu/audits/refactor.md — Refactoring plan (Fowler + Mikado)

> Report-only plan generated by huu's Refactor Plan pipeline. No code was rewritten.
> Methodologies: Fowler refactoring catalog (https://refactoring.com/catalog/), Mikado method (https://www.manning.com/books/the-mikado-method).

## 1. Baseline
- Test runner: <command>
- Baseline status: <green | red | none>
- Tests passing: <N> / <total>
- Baseline run timestamp: <ISO 8601>

## 2. Code smells catalog (per-file fan-out)
(filled in by step 2)

## 3. Top 5 targets
(filled in by step 3)

## 4. Mikado graph
(filled in by step 4)

## 5. Recommended Fowler refactorings
(filled in by step 5)

## 6. Validation
(filled in by the final step after the judge approves)

=== STEP 4 — Initialize .huu/audits/refactor-faq.json ===
Schema:
```json
{ "summary": "<=256>", "knowledge": "<=5000>", "path": "<file>", "smell": "long-function|duplication|primitive-obsession|feature-envy|data-clumps|switch-statement|shotgun-surgery|divergent-change|inappropriate-intimacy|comments-as-deodorant|other", "severity": "info|warn|critical", "fowler_refactoring": "<catalog entry name or null>" }
```
Optional per-entry prioritization fields (additive — entries without them remain valid): "priority": 1|2|3 (1 = do first), "fixability": "trivial|moderate|involved".

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify production source code in this pipeline.
- The deliverable is a plan, not a refactor. Safe refactoring requires step-by-step human review.

steps.name: 2. Select smell-catalog targets
steps.prompt:
You are huu's code-smell catalog target selector. Goal: choose the files worth the work that follows and hand them to the parallel fan-out — ONE cognitive op: pick the files, write the list, nothing else.

=== SKIP RULE (never list these) ===
Generated/vendored/trivial paths: `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `build/`, `out/`, `coverage/`, `.git/`, `vendor/`, `target/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.generated.*`, `*.min.js`, `*.min.css`, `*.d.ts`, `*.lock`, `*.snap`; and pure config / constants / type-only / barrel-export files.

=== SELECT (at most 30, most valuable first) ===
A file earns a slot only when cataloging Fowler-classic code smells in it would catch real regressions or surface real findings. Prefer:
- long files / god modules and large classes mixing many responsibilities
- files with duplicated logic, deep nesting, long parameter lists, or feature envy
- the churny core (frequently changed, high-traffic) over leaf utilities or generated code
Fewer, load-bearing files beat a long shallow list. Rank by value and let the cap drop the tail.

=== WRITE THE MEMORY FILE ===
Write your selection to the file named in the MEMORY CONTRACT appended below (huu fills in the exact path + format). One entry per file:
- `path`: repo-relative.
- `hint`: name the suspected smells here (long function, large class, duplicated code, feature envy, long parameter list) — this becomes the next agent's `$hint` and is the single most valuable thing you hand over. Never leave it empty.
- `priority`: higher runs first; the cap drops the lowest. Rank deliberately.
An EMPTY list is valid and means "nothing here qualifies" — the next stage then runs zero tasks (do not pad to hit the cap).

=== HARD RULES ===
- Selection only: do NOT modify source, run long builds, or write anything except the memory file.
- Be language-agnostic — do not assume Node.js.
- Every path you list must exist in the working tree (the consumer drops unresolvable paths).

=== BEFORE YOU START ===
Read `./.huu/audits/refactor.md` section 1 — the first step captured the test baseline there. This step writes ONLY the target list; the per-file code-smells catalog that follows does the actual finding.

steps.name: 3. Code-smells catalog: $file
steps.prompt:
You are the code-smells catalog for ONE file: `$file`. You are one of many agents running in parallel; your whole job is this single file. Goal: identify Fowler-classic code smells in this ONE file and append findings to `.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json`. NO code changes.

The recon step chose this file deliberately and left you a lead — start there: $hint

=== STEP 0 — SKIP RULE ===
SKIP IMMEDIATELY (no findings, no FAQ append) if `$file` matches: `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `build/`, `out/`, `coverage/`, `.git/`, `vendor/`, `target/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.generated.*`, `*.min.js`, `*.min.css`, `*.d.ts`, `*.lock`, `*.snap`.

=== Smell catalog (Fowler) ===
For each smell found, suggest the matching refactoring from https://refactoring.com/catalog/:

| Smell | Refactoring suggestion |
|---|---|
| Long function (>50 LOC) | Extract Function, Decompose Conditional, Replace Temp with Query |
| Long parameter list (>5 params) | Introduce Parameter Object, Preserve Whole Object, Replace Parameter with Query |
| Large class (file > 500 LOC) | Extract Class, Extract Superclass, Replace Conditional with Polymorphism |
| Duplication | Extract Function, Pull Up Method, Form Template Method |
| Primitive obsession (string/int instead of dedicated type) | Replace Primitive with Object, Replace Type Code with Class |
| Feature envy (method uses another class's data more than its own) | Move Function, Extract Function |
| Data clumps (same group of fields/params appears together) | Extract Class, Introduce Parameter Object |
| Switch / long if-else chain on a type code | Replace Conditional with Polymorphism, Replace Type Code with Subclasses |
| Shotgun surgery (one change requires editing many files) | Move Function, Move Field, Inline Class |
| Divergent change (one file changes for many reasons) | Extract Class, Split Phase |
| Inappropriate intimacy (two classes know too much about each other) | Move Function, Move Field, Hide Delegate, Change Bidirectional to Unidirectional |
| Comments-as-deodorant (a comment explaining what the next line does suggests the code itself is unclear) | Extract Function, Rename Variable, Introduce Assertion |

=== STEP 1 — Read inputs ===
- `./.huu/audits/refactor.md` (scaffold).
- `./.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json` (may be empty).
- `$file`.

=== STEP 2 — Scan for smells ===
For each smell instance found:
1. Pinpoint the location (function name + line range).
2. Identify the Fowler refactoring that addresses it.
3. Append:
```json
{ "summary": "$file:<funcName> long function (78 LOC) — Extract Function", "knowledge": "<context: what the function does, why it's grown, which extracted sub-functions would emerge>", "path": "$file", "smell": "long-function", "severity": "warn|critical", "fowler_refactoring": "Extract Function" }
```

Severity:
- info: minor (e.g. 5-line duplication).
- warn: clear violation (long function >50 LOC, params >5).
- critical: pervasive (file >500 LOC, function >150 LOC, switch chain on type code).

=== STEP 3 — Skip if `$file` is generated / vendored / config ===
Skip files matching: `*.generated.*`, `*.min.js`, `dist/*`, `build/*`, `vendor/*`, `node_modules/*`, generated migrations, type definitions `*.d.ts`.

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify `$file` or any other file.
- Append-only to FAQ.
- Re-read FAQ before each append (parallel agents).

steps.name: 4. Pick top 5 refactoring targets
steps.prompt:
You are at step 3 — pick the top 5 refactoring targets. Goal: read `.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json`, rank smells by SMELL WEIGHT × CHURN (the hotspot method), and write section "3. Top 5 targets" of `.huu/audits/refactor.md`.

=== WHY CHURN MATTERS ===
A smelly file nobody touches costs nothing; a smelly file changed weekly taxes every change. Tornhill's hotspot method (churn × complexity) is the evidence-based prioritizer — CodeScene's field data shows prioritized hotspots are ~5.5% of a codebase yet concentrate ~23% of fixed bugs. Reference: "Your Code as a Crime Scene" (2nd ed.).

=== STEP 1 — Read all findings ===
- `./.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json`.
- `./.huu/audits/refactor.md`.

=== STEP 2 — Mine churn ===
```bash
git log --format=format: --name-only --since=12.month \
  | grep -v '^$' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr \
  > ./.huu/audits/.tmp/refactor/churn.txt
```
(Drop `--since` when the repo is younger than 12 months.)

=== STEP 3 — Rank ===
Per file:
- smell_weight = 5 × critical + 2 × warn (info = 0).
- churn = commit count from step 2 (0 when absent from the log).
- target_score = smell_weight × (1 + churn / max_churn) — a churned file scores up to 2× the same smells in a dormant file.

Top 5 files by target_score = the top 5 targets. Within each, pick the single highest-severity smell as the headline. Break ties by "priority" (1 first) then "fixability" (trivial first) when the findings carry those fields.

=== STEP 4 — Write section "3. Top 5 targets" ===
Replace the placeholder with:

```
Ranking method: smell weight × churn (hotspot prioritization — Tornhill).
| # | File | Headline smell | Fowler refactoring | Smells in file | Churn (12mo) | Score | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | src/orchestrator/runner.ts | Long function (210 LOC) | Extract Function | 8 (3 critical / 5 warn) | 87 | 38.2 | high |
| 2 | ... | | | | | | |
```

Impact heuristic:
- high: > 3 critical smells in the same file OR top-decile churn.
- medium: 1–3 critical OR > 5 warns.
- low: only warns and low churn.

=== Cleanup ===
Delete `./.huu/audits/.tmp/refactor/churn.txt` after writing the section.

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify source code. Read-only git commands only.
- DO NOT alter findings in the FAQ.
- Update ONLY section "3" of `.huu/audits/refactor.md`.

steps.name: 5. Build Mikado graph per target
steps.prompt:
You are at step 4 — build a Mikado graph for the top 5 targets. Goal: for each of the top 5 targets, write the dependency graph that turns the refactor into a series of leaf-first safe steps. Populate section "4. Mikado graph" of `.huu/audits/refactor.md`.

=== STATIC Mikado planning (NOT the dynamic method) ===
The classic Mikado method (Ola Ellnestam / Daniel Brolund) is empirical:
1. State the GOAL.
2. Try the naive change.
3. Observe what breaks; each breakage becomes a PREREQUISITE.
4. REVERT and visit prerequisites leaf-first.
5. Apply the goal only once every prerequisite is green.

This pipeline runs in REPORT-ONLY mode — we cannot try-and-revert against the user's repo. We produce a STATIC Mikado-style graph instead: REASON about likely breakages based on call sites, imports, and tests, and document them as prerequisites. The output is a planning artifact, not a verified safe-to-apply graph. Acknowledge this honestly in the report.

=== STEP 1 — Read inputs ===
- `./.huu/audits/refactor.md` (section "3. Top 5 targets" tells you what to plan for).
- `./.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json`.

=== STEP 2 — For each of the top 5 targets ===
Without modifying any code, REASON about what the naive refactor would touch:
- What other modules import the symbol being refactored?
- What tests exercise this code?
- What hidden coupling exists (shared state, side effects, ordering assumptions)?

Each of those becomes a prerequisite. List them.

=== STEP 3 — Write section "4. Mikado graph" ===
For each target, an ASCII Mikado tree:

```
Target 1: Extract Function for src/orchestrator/runner.ts:runStage
└─ goal: split runStage(220 LOC) into 4 named sub-functions
   ├─ prerequisite 1: add unit tests covering runStage's 3 branches (currently only integration tests)
   │  ├─ prerequisite 1a: mock the AgentFactory in the unit tests (currently real)
   │  └─ prerequisite 1b: extract the "stage timeout" constant so tests can use a small value
   ├─ prerequisite 2: rename internal variable `s` → `stage` (it's used 47 times; bigger touch than expected)
   └─ prerequisite 3: move stage-result aggregation out of runStage and into runStageResult helper

Order of execution: leaf-first (1a, 1b, 2, 3, then 1, then goal).
```

Repeat for targets 2–5.

=== STEP 4 — Strangler Fig hint (optional) ===
If any target is a LARGE refactor that touches > 5 files, suggest a Strangler Fig approach: introduce a façade module that the rest of the codebase imports, redirect old call sites to the new module incrementally, then delete the original. Link: https://martinfowler.com/bliki/StranglerFigApplication.html.

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify source code.
- DO NOT execute any naive refactor — Mikado method is reason-first, code-second; this pipeline never gets to the code-second step.
- Update ONLY section "4".

steps.name: 6. Final Fowler recommendation list
steps.prompt:
You are the final agent — step 5. Goal: synthesize `.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json` and the Mikado graphs into a final recommendation list, populate section "5. Recommended Fowler refactorings" of `.huu/audits/refactor.md`.

=== STEP 1 — Read inputs ===
- `./.huu/audits/refactor.md` (sections 1–4 already populated).
- `./.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json` (all findings).

=== STEP 2 — Aggregate findings by Fowler refactoring ===
Group ALL findings by their `fowler_refactoring` field. For each unique refactoring (e.g. "Extract Function", "Replace Conditional with Polymorphism"), count how many findings reference it.

=== STEP 3 — Write section "5. Recommended Fowler refactorings" ===
Sort by count descending. Within each severity group, order items by "priority" (1 first) then "fixability" (trivial first) when the findings carry those fields; findings without them keep their relative order.
For each refactoring:

### Extract Function (12 occurrences across 8 files)
Catalog: https://refactoring.com/catalog/extractFunction.html
Why it helps here: most of the long-function smells in this codebase are functions that mix multiple concerns. Extract Function is the workhorse for this.
Top file pointers:
- src/orchestrator/runner.ts:42 — runStage()
- src/lib/git-preflight.ts:118 — validateRepo()
- ...

### Replace Conditional with Polymorphism (3 occurrences)
Catalog: https://refactoring.com/catalog/replaceConditionalWithPolymorphism.html
...

=== STEP 4 — Final summary block ===
At the bottom of section "5":

```
Total smells: <N>
Critical: <X>
Warnings: <Y>
Unique Fowler refactorings recommended: <Z>
Recommended starting point: <target #1 from section 3>
Recommended pace: 1 leaf per PR (Mikado discipline); never bundle prerequisite + goal in one PR.
```

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify any source code.
- DO NOT add a "Refactor everything now" recommendation — Mikado discipline is leaf-by-leaf.
- The final report must be self-contained (a human can read it without rerunning the audit).
- The counts you cite MUST match the FAQ entries exactly — the validation judge recounts them.

steps.name: 7. Validate report
steps.condition: The report at `.huu/audits/refactor.md` is complete and internally consistent. Verify ALL of: 1) the file exists and every required section ("1. Baseline", "2. Code smells catalog (per-file fan-out)", "3. Top 5 targets", "4. Mikado graph", "5. Recommended Fowler refactorings") is present and contains real content — no "(filled in by step N)" or TODO placeholders left. 2) `.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json` parses as a JSON array and the counts cited in the report's summary tables match the actual entries (severity totals, category totals). 3) recommendations are ordered critical → warn → info, and within each severity respect the optional "priority"/"fixability" fields when present. 4) `git status --porcelain` shows NO modified files outside `.huu/` except at most one `.gitignore` whose diff only rewrites a `.huu/` exclusion into `.huu/*` + a negation line (report-only contract). 5) every top-5 target from section 3 has its own Mikado graph in section 4, each graph ends with an explicit leaf-first execution order, and section 4 keeps the STATIC-plan caveat (the graphs were reasoned, not try-and-revert verified). This is run $runs of this validation. If every clause holds, answer "approved"; if any fails, answer "rework" and say precisely which clause failed and why in the reason.

steps.name: 8. Finalize report
steps.prompt:
You are the final agent — step 7 (post-validation). The judge approved the plan. Goal: stamp section "6. Validation" of `.huu/audits/refactor.md` and leave the working tree clean.

=== STEP 1 — Stamp the validation section ===
Replace the "## 6. Validation" placeholder with:
```
Validated: <UTC timestamp> · smells: <total> (critical <X> / warn <Y> / info <Z>) · baseline: <green|red|none> · sections 1–5 complete.
Mikado caveat preserved: the graphs are STATIC plans (reasoned, not try-and-revert verified).
Validation gate: judge CheckStep ("approved" required to reach this step; "rework" loops back to the recommendation list, max 2 judge runs).
```
Numbers MUST come from re-reading `.huu/audits/refactor-faq.json` and the report — do not invent them. Be idempotent: if the section is already stamped (a rework loop ran twice), overwrite it with fresh numbers.

=== STEP 2 — Exit hygiene ===
- Delete any leftovers under `./.huu/audits/.tmp/refactor/` (keep the directory).
- Confirm `git status --porcelain` shows changes only under `.huu/` (plus at most the permitted `.gitignore` adjustment).

=== HARD RULES ===
- DO NOT modify the findings, the graphs, or the recommendations — stamp only.
- DO NOT touch production files.
